statusLine: Americas crude markets remain elevated near $91 WTI amid persistent Middle East maritime risks and declining US inventories, though retail fuel prices show modest relief.
fullAnalysis:
US crude markets gained 1.12% week-on-week in the period ending June 2nd, with WTI reaching $91.47/barrel as commercial crude inventories fell by a significant 9.1 million barrels to 365.1 million barrels. This drawdown, combined with declines in gasoline (-2.6 MB) and distillate (-2.1 MB) stocks, reflects robust domestic demand against steady production of 13.7 million barrels per day. The Strategic Petroleum Reserve remains at historically low levels of 81.2 million barrels.

The WTI-Brent spread widened to -$2.64, indicating continued pressure on Americas crude pricing relative to international benchmarks. This discount reflects pipeline capacity constraints limiting export potential from key US production hubs, particularly as shale output remains strong. Canadian heavy crude (WCS) likely faces even steeper discounts given pipeline bottlenecks to US refineries.

Critical maritime security threats persist across key global shipping routes, with multiple MARAD advisories warning of Houthi attacks in the Red Sea/Bab el Mandeb corridor and Iranian threats in the Persian Gulf. While these chokepoints don't directly affect Americas-to-Americas flows, they're supporting global crude prices by disrupting Middle East-to-Europe routes and forcing longer voyage times around Africa.

Retail fuel prices provided modest consumer relief, with gasoline declining 1.6 cents to $4.605/gallon and diesel dropping 7.3 cents to $5.523/gallon. However, both remain well above historical norms, reflecting the elevated crude environment and ongoing refinery margin pressures from tight global product markets.
keyPoints: WTI crude gained 1.12% week-on-week to $91.47/barrel amid declining US commercial inventories
US crude stocks fell 9.1 million barrels while gasoline and distillate inventories also declined significantly
Multiple critical maritime advisories remain active for Red Sea and Persian Gulf shipping routes
WTI-Brent spread at -$2.64 indicates continued export constraints from US production centers
Retail gasoline and diesel prices declined modestly but remain elevated at $4.61 and $5.52 per gallon
US crude production steady at 13.7 million barrels per day with Strategic Petroleum Reserve at 81.2 million barrels
